original_hypothesis.title: FCL.ecAff_isOnCurve Accepts Non-Canonical P-256 Points Enabling Persistent Owner Backdoor
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: FCL.ecAff_isOnCurve does not reject coordinates x > p or y > p (except exact equality x == p or y == p). Since all arithmetic uses mulmod with p, a point (x+p, y) is mathematically identical to (x, y) but encodes to different bytes via abi.encode. MultiOwnable.addOwnerPublicKey stores the raw bytes without canonicalization, and _addOwnerAtIndex only checks exact byte equality for deduplication (AlreadyOwner). An existing owner can register equivalent non-canonical public keys as separate owners. When one is removed via removeOwnerAtIndex, the other persists, allowing the removed owner to retain full wallet control.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This combines a missing input validation in the cryptographic library with an encoding-level deduplication gap in the owner registry to create a persistent backdoor that survives explicit owner removal, potentially draining all wallet funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ecAff_isOnCurve rejects x==p and y==p but NOT x>p or y>p; mulmod(x,x,p) reduces x mod p, so non-canonical coordinates pass the curve check

preconditions: Attacker is an existing wallet owner (EOA or passkey)
Attacker generates a P-256 keypair where x < 2^256 - p (approximately x < 2^224, achievable with ~2^32 key generation attempts)
No external monitoring system checks for modular equivalence of newly added public keys

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: ecAff_isOnCurve checks (0==x)&&(0==y) || x==p || y==p, but mulmod(x,x,p) treats x and x+p identically for all x where x+p < 2^256
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: addOwnerPublicKey(x,y) then addOwnerPublicKey(x+p,y) creates two owner entries for the same logical key; removeOwnerAtIndex only deletes one
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: After apparent removal, attacker retains full wallet control → can drain all wallet assets via execute()

economic_questions: What fraction of P-256 key space allows non-canonical representation (x < 2^256 - p)?
Can the attacker also exploit y-coordinate non-canonicality (y < 2^256 - p)?
Are emitted AddOwner events sufficient for detection by monitoring systems?
validation_requirements: Generate a P-256 keypair with x < 2^224 and verify ecAff_isOnCurve(x+p, y) returns true
Confirm ecdsa_verify produces identical results for (x,y) and (x+p,y) with the same signature
Test that _addOwnerAtIndex accepts both (x,y) and (x+p,y) without AlreadyOwner revert
Verify removeOwnerAtIndex on one index leaves the other active

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Zero capital (only gas for addOwnerPublicKey calls, ~0.001 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.002 ETH in gas for two addOwnerPublicKey transactions
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Entire wallet balance (could be arbitrarily large)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near infinite ROI on gas cost
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Two transactions to set up backdoor; exploitation any time after apparent removal

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Before any trust-removal event; backdoor is planted preemptively

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All co-owners and wallet asset holders
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire wallet balance across all asset types

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: p = 0xFFFFFFFF00000001...FFFFFFFF; 2^256 - p ≈ 2^224; ~1/2^32 of P-256 x-coordinates qualify
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify mulmod equivalence for canonical vs non-canonical coordinates

validation_result.mechanism: Non-canonical P-256 public key coordinates bypass deduplication and precompile strictness
validation_result.impact_summary: An attacker can register a mathematically equivalent but byte-different passkey to plant a persistent backdoor that survives owner removal, allowing them to drain all wallet funds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability stems from a combination of three factors across different contracts. First, `FCL.ecAff_isOnCurve` uses `mulmod` and `addmod` for arithmetic, which implicitly reduce inputs modulo `p`. It explicitly rejects `x == p` and `y == p`, but fails to reject `x > p` or `y > p`. Second, `MultiOwnable._addOwnerAtIndex` deduplicates owners by checking strict byte equality (`isOwnerBytes`). Because `abi.encode(x, y)` and `abi.encode(x + p, y)` produce different byte strings, an attacker can register both as separate owners. Third, `WebAuthn.verify` attempts to use the RIP-7212 precompile but falls back to `FCL.ecdsa_verify` if the `staticcall` returns `success == false`. If the precompile is deployed and strictly reverts on `x >= p`, `success` will be false, triggering the fallback to FCL, which accepts the non-canonical coordinates. An attacker can generate a keypair where `x < 2^256 - p` (achievable in minutes), register both the canonical and non-canonical public keys, and retain full wallet control after the canonical key is removed.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The evidence clearly supports the hypothesis. `FCL.sol` lines 78-89 show `ecAff_isOnCurve` missing the `x >= p` check. `MultiOwnable.sol` lines 167-173 show `_addOwnerAtIndex` relying on `isOwnerBytes` for deduplication, which checks exact byte matches. `WebAuthn.sol` lines 105-110 show the fallback logic executing `FCL.ecdsa_verify` if `success` is false, effectively bypassing any strict validation enforced by the RIP-7212 precompile.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function fails to reject coordinates where `x > p` or `y > p`. Because `mulmod` and `addmod` implicitly reduce the inputs modulo `p`, non-canonical coordinates like `x + p` will pass the curve check and behave identically to `x` in subsequent ECDSA verification steps.

Deduplication relies on strict byte equality. `abi.encode(x, y)` and `abi.encode(x + p, y)` produce different byte strings, allowing an attacker to register multiple equivalent public keys without triggering the `AlreadyOwner` revert.

If the RIP-7212 precompile is deployed and strictly reverts on `x >= p`, `success` will be false. The code skips the `if` statement and falls back to `FCL.ecdsa_verify`, which accepts the non-canonical coordinates. This makes the vulnerability exploitable even on chains with a strictly compliant precompile.

calling_sequence: WebAuthn.verify(challenge, requireUV, webAuthnAuth, x + p, y)
FCL.ecdsa_verify(messageHash, r, s, x + p, y)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: An attacker generates a keypair where `x < 2^256 - p` and calls `addOwnerPublicKey(x + p, y)`. The `_addOwnerAtIndex` function accepts it because the byte encoding differs from the canonical `(x, y)` key. Later, the attacker submits a `UserOperation` signed with this key. `_validateSignature` decodes `x + p` and passes it to `WebAuthn.verify`. The precompile `staticcall` either returns empty (if not deployed) or reverts (if deployed and strictly checking `x < p`), causing `success && valid` to be false. Execution falls back to `FCL.ecdsa_verify`, which calls `ecAff_isOnCurve`. The `mulmod` operations reduce `x + p` to `x`, passing the curve check and successfully verifying the signature.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided, but the vulnerability is intrinsic to the contract logic and independent of configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is highly realistic. Generating a P-256 keypair where `x < 2^224` requires approximately `2^32` attempts, which takes only a few minutes on modern hardware. The attacker registers both `(x, y)` and `(x + p, y)`. When the canonical owner is removed (e.g., by a co-owner or an admin), the non-canonical owner remains active. The attacker can then drain the wallet at any time.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. This vulnerability allows an existing owner to plant an undetectable, persistent backdoor that survives explicit removal. The attacker can subsequently execute arbitrary transactions, including draining all assets held by the wallet.
recommended_mitigations: In `FCL.sol`, update `ecAff_isOnCurve` to explicitly reject coordinates greater than or equal to `p`: `if (x >= p || y >= p || ((0 == x) && (0 == y))) return false;`
In `MultiOwnable.sol`, add validation in `addOwnerPublicKey` to ensure `uint256(x) < FCL.p` and `uint256(y) < FCL.p` before encoding and storing the owner.
In `WebAuthn.sol`, modify the fallback logic to only fall back if the precompile is not deployed (e.g., by checking `VERIFIER.code.length == 0`), rather than falling back on any `staticcall` failure.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Write a script to generate P-256 keypairs until `x < 2^256 - p` (approx `x < 2^224`). 2. Deploy `CoinbaseSmartWallet` and initialize it with the canonical public key `(x, y)`. 3. Call `addOwnerPublicKey(x + p, y)` to register the non-canonical key. 4. Call `removeOwnerAtIndex` to remove the canonical key `(x, y)`. 5. Construct a `UserOperation` to transfer funds, signed with the private key, and set `ownerIndex` to the index of `(x + p, y)`. 6. Submit the `UserOperation` to `validateUserOp` and observe it returning 0 (success).
